reasoning:
The baseline number of protests in Curacao in the past 360 days (plus one) currently averages to about 1.167 protests per month. For the question to resolve positively, the 30-day count leading up to the resolution date would have to be more than ten times this number, which equates to 11.67 or more (i.e., at least 12 protests in a 30-day window). Curacao has a population of ~150,000 and is generally politically stable, with protests historically being quite rare — the major exception in recent times was a brief spike in June 2020 due to pandemic-related economic austerity. There are extremely few protests normally, typically fewer than 2 a year.

Recent news does not indicate any major ongoing labor disputes, election crises, or local political turmoil that would spawn almost a dozen separate protest events in a 1-month period. A January 2026 travel advisory indicated Curacao remained a safe 'Level 1' destination experiencing normal stability. 

While the arrest of Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro by US forces in January 2026 presents an external shock that could theoretically provoke regional demonstrations/diaspora protests, 12 discrete ACLED-coded protest events within a 30-day window in a tiny island nation is an extraordinarily high bar. Curacao's standard protest rhythm simply doesn't sustain that density of distinct events under nearly any normal conditions. To be thorough, uncertainty naturally rises around the later outcome date if the situation somehow escalates out of nowhere, but base rates heavily penalize 12+ protests in 30 days. P remains solidly low.
